pair_convention.spot_explanation: USD/JPY at 156.68 means 1 USD = 156.68 JPY. A rising pair means the USD is strengthening against the JPY (yen weakening — more yen required per dollar). A falling pair means the USD is weakening against the JPY (yen strengthening — fewer yen required per dollar). All bullish/bearish references in this report are expressed relative to this convention.
pair_convention.bullish_means: pair RISES (USD strengthens vs JPY / yen weakens)
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (USD weakens vs JPY / yen strengthens)

executive_summary: USD/JPY closed at 156.68 on 9 May 2026, trading in the 81st percentile of its 52-week range (139.88–160.72) after a dramatic ¥5.4 trillion (~$34.5bn) MOF intervention on 1 May knocked the pair from above 160 to below 156 within hours. Over the past month the pair is down 1.22% (yen stronger), though it remains up 5.46% over one year reflecting the persistent rate differential. The dominant bilateral narrative is a tug-of-war between the still-wide Fed-BOJ policy gap of ~287bp — which structurally supports USD/JPY — and a converging trajectory driven by the most hawkish BOJ vote split since 2016 (6-3 with three members dissenting for an immediate hike to 1.00%) colliding with a fractured FOMC (8-4) whose dovish bias is intensifying under stagflationary pressure. The Iran ceasefire unwind is removing energy-shock support for the pair, oil's correction from $106 to ~$95 partially healing Japan's terms-of-trade damage, while intervention has established a credible 160 ceiling. Both dossiers are independently bearish USD and bullish JPY, creating an unusually reinforcing bilateral signal for USD/JPY to fall. Our headline stance is Bearish USD/JPY (pair falls) with tactical conviction 3/5 and strategic conviction 4/5 — targeting 152–154 over 2–4 weeks and 148–152 over 1–6 months, with the June 17 BOJ MPM and FOMC as the critical inflection events.

market_context.valuation_assessment: PPP-implied fair value for USD/JPY is approximately 95 (OECD) — the pair trades ~65% above fair value, placing JPY among the most undervalued G10 currencies in history. The BIS REER for JPY sits in the bottom decile of post-1990 history. This extreme deviation is a structural anchor for long-term yen appreciation but is not tactically actionable given the carry incentive to stay long USD/JPY. The pair's valuation extreme implies significant mean-reversion potential over multi-year horizons but has been sustained by the persistent yield differential.

positioning.base_cftc_net: Aggregate G10 short-USD positioning is the most extreme in over a decade
positioning.quote_cftc_net: Leveraged funds net long JPY at +56,400 contracts (28 April); asset managers net short JPY at -26,550 contracts
positioning.net_pair_implication: Both legs are positioned for USD/JPY to fall — leveraged funds long JPY and aggregate G10 short USD. This removes the historic short-squeeze catalyst (peak shorts were -180,000 in 2024) but means the downside trade is crowded. A hot 12 May CPI print could trigger a 200–300 pip USD/JPY spike as the short-USD leg is squeezed.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Pair trade crowded on short side — both legs positioned for pair to fall, limiting fresh bearish fuel but constraining upside squeeze potential

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50-day SMA: 158.88 (spot below), 100-day SMA: 157.63 (spot below), 200-day SMA: 156.44 (spot just above at 156.68). Configuration is mildly bearish with pair trading in the tangled zone between MAs; decisive break below 200-day would confirm bearish trend resumption.
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Bearish ascending wedge from the 1 May low of 155.55 has broken down, reinforcing the start of another bearish impulsive down move sequence within its ongoing minor downtrend phase. The short-term trend is bearish below the 157.30/55 pivotal resistance.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV exceeds IV by approximately 2.5 vol points, meaning directional spot trades are being rewarded over options strategies; realised volatility remains elevated due to the 1 May intervention spike
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Risk reversals have shifted toward JPY-call premium (USD/JPY put bid) post-intervention, reflecting hedging demand for yen upside. The skew favours downside USD/JPY protection.
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Modest backwardation through the June BOJ MPM and FOMC cluster, reflecting event risk pricing for the 17 June decision pair
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Mid-range / compressing from wartime spikes of ~14%

market_context.recent_regime: The pair has been defined by three forces over the past 30 days: (1) the MOF intervention that capped the topside at 160.72 and established the 155–160 trading corridor; (2) the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish hold that repriced June hike probability to ~35–40%; and (3) the Iran ceasefire emergence that is pulling oil lower and reducing Japan's terms-of-trade penalty. ING characterised the May 1 intervention as the start of 'Japan's 2026 FX intervention campaign,' noting USD/JPY was trading above 160 heading into the May Day holidays, echoing the 2024 pattern. The 160 level has become a clear line in the sand for Japanese authorities, with repeated tests now actively defended.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move ~35 pips against before carry is erased over 1M (trivially exceeded by any single intervention episode or BOJ surprise)
bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The annualised carry for a long USD/JPY position is approximately +287bp (Fed Funds midpoint 3.625% minus BOJ 0.75%). At a spot of 156.68, this translates to roughly 1.25 pips of daily carry, or ~35 pips over a 30-day period. With 1M IV at ~9.5%, the carry-to-vol ratio is approximately 0.30 — historically unattractive compared to the 0.50+ threshold that typically sustains carry-trade accumulation. The carry regime is structurally weakening: the Japanese yen carry trade returns are falling as the US-Japan gap compresses through 2026, while risks are rising from intervention risk, more FX sensitivity to BOJ language, and a steeper JGB curve. The carry faces a structural headwind because the BOJ is raising the borrowing cost while the Fed is reducing the return — that tug-of-war defines the 2026 outlook.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Fed-BOJ policy rate gap of ~287bp (3.625% vs 0.75%) remains the widest in the G10 complex and the primary structural support for USD/JPY. However, the trajectory is decisively narrowing: the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish vote on 28 April (three dissenters for an immediate hike to 1.00%) is the most fractured BOJ decision under Ueda, while the FOMC's 8-4 split on 29 April signals a Fed that is losing institutional consensus. The US-Japan 10Y spread has compressed to ~186bp (US 4.36% vs JGB 2.50%, the latter the highest since July 1997), and the direction is clear: BOJ hikes while Fed holds-to-cuts. OIS-implied pricing shows ~35–40% probability of a BOJ June hike, which we assess as too low given three board members already at 1.00%, while the Fed is pricing ~80% hold in June with one cut by year-end. The bilateral carry at 287bp is still meaningful but the carry-to-vol ratio of ~0.30 is unattractive, meaning carry is not compensating for the directional risk.
key_bilateral_metrics: Carry-to-vol ratio: 0.30 (below 0.50 threshold for carry attractiveness)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing a slow, one-hike-per-meeting BOJ normalisation path with the next move in July. The edge lies in the June hike being underpriced — a surprise June hike would shock USD/JPY 200–300 pips lower.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the bilateral rate-convergence dynamic is the dominant driver and it overwhelmingly favours USD/JPY declining. The projected end-2026 US-Japan policy gap compresses from ~287bp to ~225bp as the BOJ reaches 1.00–1.25% (MUFG, Sony Financial) and the Fed delivers 25–50bp of cuts. These projections assume BOJ rates reach 1.00–1.25% by late 2026, and every 100bp of compression has historically correlated with a 5–8 yen move. The US-Japan 10Y spread should narrow toward 140–170bp as JGB 10Y rises toward 2.7% and US 10Y eases toward 4.0–4.2%. This compression transmits to USD/JPY through three channels: (a) reduced carry attractiveness deterring fresh longs; (b) Japanese institutional repatriation flows as hedging costs improve below 150; (c) systematic model signals turning bearish as forward curves steepen.
key_bilateral_metrics: ING targets 152 by year-end and 148 forecast for year-end 2026
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus forecasts project a trading range of roughly 146–154 for 2026, with year-end expectations clustering in that zone. Strategist forecasts span from J.P. Morgan's bullish 164 target to more moderate expectations around 151–157. We expect the lower end of the range, targeting 148–152.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons are bearish but strategic confidence (75) exceeds tactical (70) because the multi-meeting BOJ normalisation path and Fed easing trajectory provide more events for convergence to materialise over 1–6 months versus the single-meeting binary at the tactical horizon.

carry_analysis.structural_headwind: BOJ raising borrowing cost while Fed reducing return — tug-of-war defines 2026 outlook

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral inflation-growth dynamic is shifting in JPY's favour. US stagflation is intensifying: headline CPI +3.3% with core PCE +3.2% while Q1 GDP disappointed at +2.0% vs 2.3% consensus with the GDP price index spiking to +4.5%. Japan's growth is weaker (FY2026 GDP cut to 0.5%) but inflation is rising in the direction that forces BOJ action — core CPI accelerated to 1.8% in March with core-core at 2.4%, and Shunto 2026 delivered a 5.09% wage hike locking in a structural inflation floor. The bilateral data surprise differential favours JPY: US data is running below consensus (Citi Surprise Index modestly negative) while the BOJ's upward revision of FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% suggests the Bank itself expects inflation to remain above target. The key asymmetry is that US stagflation forces cuts (weakening USD) while Japan's inflation acceleration forces hikes (strengthening JPY) — the policy-response functions reinforce the same directional signal.
key_bilateral_metrics: US core PCE: 3.2% vs Japan core CPI: 1.8% (gap narrowing as Japan's rises)
US Q1 GDP: +2.0% vs Japan Q4 GDP: +0.3% QoQ (both slowing)
Michigan 1Y expectations: 4.7% vs BOJ FY2026 CPI forecast: 2.8%
Shunto 2026: 5.09% wage growth (anchors Japan inflation floor)
12 May US CPI is the binary resolution catalyst
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets expect a stable US inflation print (~0.3% MoM for April CPI) and declining Japan inflation toward 1.5%. We see asymmetric risk: hot US CPI lifts USD/JPY temporarily but doesn't change the cut trajectory, while Japan's 23 May Tokyo CPI printing above 2% accelerates the BOJ June hike timeline.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, US stagflation mechanically weakens USD while Japan's wage-driven inflation cycle sustains BOJ normalisation momentum. The Fed faces a worse trade-off (cut into elevated inflation vs hold and weaken growth), while the BOJ has a cleaner path (hike because inflation is persistently above target and wages are locked in). The bilateral growth differential is less important than the inflation trajectory for this pair because it's the policy-response function that drives rates. Shunto's 5.09% wage hike is a 12-month lock-in providing structural inflation support for Japan well beyond near-term data noise, while the US faces a stagflationary trap that reinforces the Fed's dovish bias regardless of individual monthly prints.
key_bilateral_metrics: US core PCE trajectory: 2.5–3.0% through year-end (sticky)
Japan core CPI trajectory: 2.0–2.5% through year-end (rising on wage cycle)
BOJ FY2026 CPI revision: 2.8% (largest single-meeting upward revision)
Fed-BOJ response asymmetry: US cuts into inflation; Japan hikes into inflation
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus expects both inflation rates to moderate. We see persistence in both, but the policy implications point in the same direction for USD/JPY: lower.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bearish; strategic has higher confidence because the wage-cycle persistence (Shunto is a 12-month lock-in) provides structural inflation support for Japan beyond the tactical data noise.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: USD/JPY is a pure risk-proxy pair; higher beta to risk sentiment justifies +0.03 increase in both tactical and strategic weights

tactical.bilateral_analysis: USD/JPY is the G10's premier risk proxy pair, but its risk-beta has been complicated in 2026 by the energy-import overlay. During the Iran war, the yen failed to rally despite global risk-off because Japan's terms-of-trade vulnerability (95.9% Middle East crude oil dependence) dominated the safe-haven reflex. With risk-on conditions now dominant (S&P 500 at record 7,365, VIX 17.4), the standard framework says USD/JPY should rise as risk-on weakens JPY. However, the dollar-smile positions USD at the bottom (weak in goldilocks), creating an offsetting USD-negative force. The net signal is neutral: risk-on weakens JPY but also weakens USD. The Nikkei VI at ~29.5 is materially elevated vs VIX, signalling Japan-specific stress that dampens the standard risk-beta.
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price benign risk-on continuation. The pair-specific signal is muted because the dollar-smile-bottom and energy-import overlay cancel each other for USD/JPY.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, as the Iran energy shock abates and oil normalises toward $80–90, JPY's classical safe-haven correlation should reassert. Japan's $3.3T NIIP (world's largest) underpins the structural repatriation reflex during risk-off episodes. The 30% recession probability (JPM/Goldman) creates asymmetric downside for risk assets and USD/JPY via safe-haven mechanics. Over 1–6 months, we expect at least one 5–8% S&P drawdown, with USD/JPY dropping 3–5 figures on the JPY rally leg as the dollar-smile-bottom USD regime and JPY safe-haven function operate simultaneously.
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price risk-on perpetuity. The asymmetric risk for USD/JPY is a risk-off episode that simultaneously weakens USD (dollar-smile-bottom regime) and strengthens JPY (safe-haven repatriation).

factors.horizon_split: Tactical neutral reflects the energy-import overlay that dampens JPY safe-haven function; strategic bearish reflects post-oil-normalisation reactivation of the safe-haven channel.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The positioning setup is unusual: leveraged funds are net long JPY (+56,400 contracts) AND the aggregate G10 complex is net short USD — both legs are already positioned for USD/JPY to fall. This removes the historic short-squeeze catalyst that made every JPY rally violent (peak shorts were -180,000 in 2024). The absence of fresh shorts means a USD/JPY rally to 160+ would face less squeeze fuel than prior episodes, but it also means the downside trade is crowded. On the USD side, the most extreme short-USD positioning in over a decade creates a tactical squeeze risk that could temporarily lift USD/JPY on a hot 12 May CPI print. The combined bilateral signal is that the directional trade is already on, but crowding limits incremental momentum.
key_bilateral_metrics: DXY net spec: light long (rebuilt from 14-year bear extreme)
Combined bilateral signal: both legs positioned for pair to fall
Squeeze risk: 1–2% USD upside on CPI surprise → ~200–300 pip USD/JPY spike
tactical.what_is_priced: The directional trade is already on. The contrarian risk is to the upside on a hawkish US data surprise.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the contrarian signal fades as positioning has time to rebuild. The structural flow channel — Japanese institutional outflows (lifers, GPIF) — is the dominant strategic driver and it's shifting: at USD/JPY below 150, hedging costs improve and demand re-accelerates, providing structural yen-supportive flow. At 156, hedging dynamics are mixed. The IMF COFER data showing gradual USD reserve share decline (-50 to -100bp/year) is a slow but persistent structural USD-negative that adds to the directional bias over the 1–6 month horizon without providing a clear near-term catalyst.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japanese lifer foreign-bond demand activation: USD/JPY <150
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic positioning is moderately yen-supportive. No fresh bearish-JPY accumulation cycle is priced.

factors.horizon_split: Both neutral; tactical has the contrarian squeeze risk from crowded bilateral positioning, while strategic positioning signals are opaque and could surprise either way.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Both legs positioned for USD/JPY to fall — leveraged funds net long JPY (+56,400) and aggregate G10 net short USD. The directional trade is on but crowded.
tactical.unwind_trigger: Brent re-spiking above $120 (Iran ceasefire failure), BOJ June dovish hold (8-1 vote), or hot US CPI ≥+0.4% MoM — any single catalyst could unwind the long JPY position toward 0 to -50k territory, implying a USD/JPY rally of 4–7 figures
tactical.historical_analogue: Leveraged funds peaked at -180,000 net short JPY in 2024, requiring a 15+ figure unwind. Current positioning at +56,400 net long means a moderate squeeze, not a 2024-style violent reversal.
tactical.contrarian_case: If a hot 12 May CPI print coincides with hawkish Warsh inaugural commentary on 15 May, the crowded short-USD long-JPY bilateral trade could unwind simultaneously, driving USD/JPY 300–500 pips higher in a squeeze that has nothing to do with the fundamental rate-convergence thesis. This is a real tactical risk with ~25% probability given the 12 May binary.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: Strategically, positioning will normalise and the structural flow channel via Japanese institutional investors becomes dominant. GPIF and lifer repatriation dynamics at sub-150 levels provide sustained yen-supportive flow.
strategic.unwind_trigger: CFTC leveraged funds net long JPY surging above +100,000 contracts combined with USD/JPY failing to break 152 would create an overcrowded position vulnerable to reversal
strategic.historical_analogue: GPIF's 2015 shift to foreign assets drove USD/JPY from 120 to 125 in weeks; the reverse — repatriation as hedging costs improve — could drive a comparable move lower
strategic.contrarian_case: Strategic contrarian risk is to the upside if US exceptionalism narrative re-engages: USD/JPY breaking 162 sustained would attract fresh momentum longs, rebuilding the structural short position and invalidating the yen-bullish thesis. This requires a combination of hawkish Fed surprise, BOJ delay to 2027, and Iran re-ignition simultaneously — tail probability of approximately 10–15%.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral external balance net calculation is the critical differentiator for USD/JPY. The US is now a net energy exporter (~6.44 mbpd oil exports, record), while Japan imports 95.9% of crude from the Middle East — the widest bilateral energy-vulnerability gap in the G10. However, the direction of travel is yen-supportive tactically: Brent has corrected from a $126 wartime peak to ~$102, and the Iran ceasefire is pulling oil lower. Each $10/bbl decline in Brent improves Japan's terms of trade and compresses the bilateral vulnerability gap. The US current account deficit at -3.0% of GDP vs Japan's +4.0% surplus creates a structural flow differential that favours JPY structurally, though this is rarely a tactical driver. Net commodity exposure using the framework rules: Oil down → mild negative for USD (as net exporter, smaller effect), strongly positive for JPY (as massive net importer). Net effect: pair falls (bearish USD/JPY).
key_bilateral_metrics: US CA: -3.0% of GDP vs Japan CA: +4.0% of GDP = 700bp bilateral gap
Japan ME crude oil dependence: 95.9% (vs US net exporter)
Japan March trade balance: ¥+667bn (miss vs ¥+1,106bn consensus)
US NIIP: -$26T vs Japan NIIP: +$3.3T = $29.3T bilateral gap (largest in G10)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price Brent reverting toward $90 on ceasefire. We expect $85–95 over 4 weeks if ceasefire holds — each $10 below $100 adds 100–200 pips of yen-supportive flow.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the external balance dynamic dominates with 0.16 weight and strongly favours yen. Japan's +$3.3T NIIP generates persistent primary income flows (~¥45–50T annualised) that structurally support the current account regardless of trade-balance fluctuations. The US twin deficit (-5.8% fiscal + -3.0% CA = -8.8% of GDP) is the worst since the 1985 Plaza Accord and creates structural USD depreciation pressure. If the Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80 by Q3, Japan's terms of trade meaningfully improve, removing the 2026 headwind and restoring the yen's structural surplus advantage. The $29.3T bilateral NIIP gap between the US (-$26T) and Japan (+$3.3T) is the largest in the G10 complex and represents a persistent structural headwind for USD/JPY over a 1–6 month horizon.
key_bilateral_metrics: US twin deficit: -8.8% of GDP (worst since 1985)
Net bilateral commodity exposure: oil down = strongly bearish USD/JPY
strategic.what_is_priced: Bloomberg consensus (USD/JPY 153 end-2026) implicitly prices oil normalisation. We see faster TOT improvement supporting a move to 148–152.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bearish (55) as oil correction is ongoing; strategic bearish (65) as the structural external balance gap ($29.3T NIIP differential) dominates over 1–6 months.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: USD: US is a net energy exporter (~6.44 mbpd oil exports, record) — oil price declines are modestly negative for USD as export earnings decline, but the effect is small relative to monetary policy drivers
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: JPY: Japan imports 95.9% of crude oil from the Middle East — oil price declines are strongly positive for JPY via terms-of-trade improvement, current account improvement, and reduced energy import bill
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil down $10/bbl → US export revenues modestly lower → marginal USD negative (small magnitude given USD is driven by rates, not commodity exports). Step 2: Oil down $10/bbl → Japan's import bill declines materially (95.9% ME dependence) → terms of trade improve → current account improves → JPY structurally supported. Step 3: Net bilateral effect = USD mildly negative + JPY strongly positive = USD/JPY falls (pair bearish). Step 4: Oil down from $102 to $85 ($17 decline) = estimated 200–400 pip yen-supportive flow over 4–8 weeks based on historical passthrough. Magnitude: asymmetric — JPY benefits far outweigh USD costs.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Brent +10% (~$112): USD/JPY rises 200–400 pips as Japan's terms-of-trade deteriorate and the energy shock yen-weakness channel reactivates, partially offsetting rate-convergence thesis
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Brent -10% (~$92): USD/JPY falls 150–300 pips as Japan's terms-of-trade improve materially and yen safe-haven/structural demand reasserts; oil below $90 adds 100–200 additional pips
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude is overwhelmingly dominant for this pair due to Japan's near-total Middle East crude oil dependence (95.9%), creating the largest bilateral energy-vulnerability asymmetry in the G10 complex

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral guidance divergence is the sharpest in a decade. The BOJ's three-way hawkish dissent (Nakagawa, Takata, Tamura all voting for 1.00%) with Ueda explicitly stating 'real interest rates are at significantly low levels' is central-bank prose for an imminent hike. The Fed's communication is transitional — Powell's 'in a good place to move in either direction' is deliberately ambiguous, but the Powell-Warsh transition on 15 May adds institutional uncertainty. The credibility differential favours the BOJ: Ueda has been more transparent than predecessors, and the market increasingly trusts BOJ hawkish guidance. The Fed's credibility is under attack from political interference (DOJ subpoenas, Pirro appeal), while the BOJ faces no such institutional stress.
key_bilateral_metrics: BOJ April vote: 6-3 (three hawkish dissents for 1.00%)
FOMC April vote: 8-4 (most fractured since October 1992)
Powell: 'in a good place to move in either direction'
tactical.what_is_priced: BOJ June hike at ~35–40%; we estimate 45–50%. A surprise June hike would shock USD/JPY 200–300 pips lower.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the guidance divergence compounds: the BOJ's FY2026 core CPI revision to 2.8% is the largest single-meeting upward revision in BOJ history, locking in a multi-quarter normalisation path. The Fed faces institutional erosion (independence litigation, Warsh transition uncertainty, 8-4 fracture) that degrades reaction-function reliability. The combination is unambiguously bearish USD/JPY: BOJ becomes more credibly hawkish while Fed becomes less credibly hawkish. MUFG projects BOJ reaching 1.00% by Q3 and 1.25% by Q4 2026, while the Fed dot plot has no hawkish repricing room, making the June 16–17 consecutive BOJ MPM and FOMC the most impactful back-to-back central bank event cluster of the year.
key_bilateral_metrics: BOJ FY2026 CPI projection: 2.8% (largest upward revision ever)
MUFG BOJ rate path: 1.00% by Q3, 1.25% by Q4 2026
Fed dot plot vs OIS gap: ~0bp (no hawkish repricing room)
June 16–17: BOJ MPM and FOMC on consecutive days
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price slow BOJ normalisation with one hike (July). We expect two hikes (June or July + September) reaching 1.25%.

factors.horizon_split: Both bearish; strategic confidence (70) higher because the multi-meeting framework provides more opportunities for BOJ hawkish guidance to crystallise into action.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Iran ceasefire is the dominant bilateral event risk, and its trajectory is USD/JPY-negative because the war risk premium unwind hurts USD (dollar-smile-bottom reinforced) and helps JPY (terms-of-trade improvement). The MOF intervention on 1 May established a credible 160 ceiling, creating asymmetric downside for USD/JPY — the pair can fall freely but is capped on the upside by both intervention and BOJ hawkishness. Near-term events: 12 May US CPI (binary for both legs), 15 May Powell-Warsh transition, 19 May Japan Q1 GDP preliminary, 23 May Tokyo CPI. Re-ignition tail risk (~25%) would spike USD/JPY back toward 162–165 on oil-driven yen weakness.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran ceasefire probability: ~75% holding through June
12 May US CPI: binary resolution for both legs
Re-ignition tail risk: ~25% (would lift USD/JPY 5–8 figures)
tactical.what_is_priced: ~75% ceasefire probability. The re-ignition tail is underpriced given Trump's belligerent rhetoric.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the geopolitical tail risks are balanced for this pair. Iran ceasefire success (→ oil $80, JPY positive) competes with US-Japan auto tariff risk (~20% probability, JPY negative) and recession tail (30%, JPY positive via safe-haven). The US midterms (3 November) add political uncertainty that weighs on USD. Net: roughly balanced with high-variance binary outcomes. The joint tail-risk probability across all geopolitical scenarios is higher than individual pricing implies, with the distribution skewed modestly toward JPY-supportive outcomes.
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price benign outcomes. The joint tail-risk probability is higher than individual pricing implies.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bearish (ceasefire direction clear) vs strategic neutral (multiple competing tails).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral fiscal differential favours yen: the US is on a deteriorating fiscal trajectory (FY2026 deficit $1.9T / 5.8% of GDP, debt 101% of GDP, 30Y UST briefly 5.00%, weak late-March 2Y auction) while Japan's fiscal profile, though nominally worse (250% gross debt/GDP), is structurally insulated by 50% BOJ ownership of JGBs and ~90% domestic ownership. The US fiscal stress is increasingly a USD-negative factor as 30Y term premium re-rates and the $79bn Q2 borrowing revision adds supply pressure. Japan's Takaichi fiscal stimulus (¥21.3T supplementary + ¥120T budget) is yen-ambiguous: it supports growth (yen-positive) but adds supply pressure to long-end JGBs (yen-negative via fiscal concern). Net signal: mildly bearish USD/JPY because US fiscal stress is a more acute market-relevant factor.
key_bilateral_metrics: US debt/GDP: 101% (CBO, rising to 120% by 2036) vs Japan: 250% gross / 120% net
US 30Y briefly 5.00% (term premium, fiscal stress signal)
US ratings: AA+/Aa1 vs Japan: A+/A1 (Japan lower but stable)
tactical.what_is_priced: US fiscal stress is partially priced in term premium. Further weak auctions would tilt USD/JPY lower.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the US twin deficit spiral (-8.8% of GDP) is structurally USD-negative, while Japan's fiscal profile is structurally stable (despite high nominal debt) as long as the BOJ remains a dominant buyer. The 30Y UST sustained above 5.25% would signal a fiscal-driven term-premium regime that historically weakens rather than strengthens the dollar — a mechanism that would directly compress USD/JPY. The US net interest expense approaching 17% of revenue (near the 20% historical stress threshold) represents a structural constraint on US fiscal flexibility that will persist regardless of near-term political outcomes.
key_bilateral_metrics: US twin deficit: -8.8% vs Japan surplus + fiscal stimulus
US net interest 17% of revenue (approaching 20% historical threshold)
strategic.what_is_priced: Gradual US fiscal deterioration is priced. A discrete fiscal crisis (auction failure) is not.

factors.horizon_split: Both mildly bearish for USD/JPY; strategic more impactful due to the 0.07 weight and the cumulative nature of fiscal dynamics.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: From the price data, USD/JPY at 156.68 trades below the 50-day SMA (158.88) and 100-day SMA (157.63), but just above the 200-day SMA (156.44). This configuration is bearish: spot is below the first two MAs with the 200-day acting as immediate dynamic support. A decisive break below 156.44 (200-day) would confirm bearish trend resumption and trigger CTA selling. OANDA's Kelvin Wong identifies the rebound from 155.55 as 'a bearish ascending wedge' that has broken down, 'reinforcing the start of another bearish impulsive down move sequence within its ongoing minor downtrend phase.' FOREX.com identifies downside targets at 154.45, 153.00, and 150.90 if intervention-driven momentum sustains. The intervention ceiling at 160.72 caps upside risk, creating asymmetric payoff for short USD/JPY positions.
key_bilateral_metrics: 1M IV: ~9.5%; RV 20d: ~12% (RV exceeds IV by 2.5 vol pts)
Vol regime: mid-range, compressing from wartime spikes
tactical.what_is_priced: Vol markets have compressed post-intervention. The edge: USD/JPY puts are reasonably priced given the intervention-defined asymmetry.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: In the bigger picture, the corrective pattern from 161.94 (2024 high) may have been completed at 139.87, with the rise from there resuming the long-term uptrend. However, sustained break of 55-week EMA (now at 153.90) will dampen this view and bring deeper fall back towards 139.87. The strategic trend is transitioning from the 2024–25 yen-weakness regime into a potential yen-stabilisation regime. A clean break of 152 would confirm trend reversal and trigger systematic CTA flows, accelerating the move toward 148–150. The 55-week EMA at ~153.90 is the critical strategic pivot level that separates the consolidation scenario from the trend-reversal scenario.
key_bilateral_metrics: Carry-to-vol: 0.30 (carry trades will not rebuild at current levels)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price USD/JPY in a 150–160 range through year-end with bias to lower end on consensus.

factors.horizon_split: Both bearish; tactical confidence (60) higher than strategic (55) because the near-term structure is better-defined (intervention ceiling, ascending wedge breakdown, 200-day dynamic support test).

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: USD/JPY 1M IV at ~9.5% is at the 50–60th percentile over 1 year, having compressed from wartime spikes of ~14%. RV exceeds IV by approximately 2.5 vol points (RV 20d: ~12%), meaning directional spot trades are being rewarded over options strategies. The vol term structure exhibits modest backwardation through the June BOJ MPM cluster, pricing event risk.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Spot expression of short USD/JPY is more efficient than options given RV exceeds IV. The intervention-defined ceiling at 160 creates natural stop placement for shorts (stop above 161). For asymmetric tail-hedging, long 1M USD/JPY puts struck at 153–155 offer reasonable payoff into the June BOJ; risk reversals (sell 160 calls, buy 153 puts) capture the intervention asymmetry cheaply. For income generation, selling 1M 162 calls into the intervention ceiling generates yield with limited tail risk.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The June 17 BOJ MPM and FOMC on consecutive days is the dominant event risk. Vol term structure is pricing this cluster, making short-dated options (1M) ahead of that cluster relatively well-priced. Post-event vol collapse would favour spot positions over options structures after the June 17 resolution.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: USD/JPY is the second most liquid G10 FX pair (after EUR/USD) with typical bid/offer of 0.5–1.0 pip. Execution risk is minimal for institutional size. Intervention risk creates gap risk overnight in Tokyo hours — positions should account for potential 150–300 pip overnight gap.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross: reduce USD-specific factor by -0.03 (though USD is the base, carry channel is captured in Factor 1)

tactical.bilateral_analysis: USD funding conditions are abundant and stable (SOFR ~3.65–3.70%, no Fed FX swap-line draws since February, bank reserves ~$3.4T at ~13% of GDP), pushing USD to the dollar-smile bottom — the USD-negative regime. The USD/JPY-specific funding channel is structurally shifting: the JPY-funded carry trade is less attractive at compressed differentials (carry-to-vol 0.30) and elevated JPY vol, reducing structural demand for long USD/JPY. The JPY cross-currency basis is structurally negative (~-30 to -50bp historical), reflecting persistent Japanese institutional demand for USD funding — but this demand pauses at USD/JPY 155 where hedging costs bite. The Fed's dovish bias (one cut by year-end) compresses the funding advantage further, while the BOJ's hawkish trajectory raises JPY borrowing costs.
key_bilateral_metrics: JPY/USD 3M XCCY basis: structurally negative (~-30 to -50bp)
Fed FX swap line draws: ZERO (no USD funding stress)
Dollar-smile position: bottom (USD weak in goldilocks)
tactical.what_is_priced: Benign USD funding with no stress. USD/JPY carry trade is gradually being demonetised.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, as the Fed eases toward 3.25–3.50% and the BOJ hikes toward 1.00–1.25%, the structural funding-currency role of JPY weakens permanently. Each 25bp narrowing of the US-Japan gap reduces the incentive to fund positions in yen, supporting gradual yen appreciation. As JGB yields rise and forward markets price further BOJ hikes, hedging the yen becomes more expensive — this encourages a gradual unwind of carry positions. The dollar-smile-bottom regime persists strategically as the Fed eases without generating funding stress, maintaining the USD-negative backdrop that amplifies the yen-supportive rate convergence thesis.
strategic.what_is_priced: Benign funding with gradual rate convergence. The structural carry-trade deleveraging is underpriced.

factors.horizon_split: Both bearish at 60 confidence. The funding regime consistently favours USD/JPY decline across both horizons as rate convergence reduces carry attractiveness and the dollar-smile-bottom regime persists.

adjustments.reason: USD/JPY is a pure risk-proxy pair; higher beta to risk sentiment justifies +0.03 increase
Non-USD cross: reduce USD-specific factor by -0.03 (though USD is the base, carry channel captured in Factor 1)

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.483 maps to Conviction 3/5 (clear directional view). Eight of ten factors are bearish; two (Risk Appetite, Flows & Positioning) are neutral. The dominant contributors are Policy & Rates (-0.168, the single largest), Inflation & Growth (-0.108), and USD Liquidity (-0.048). The score supports a clear bearish USD/JPY directional view without reaching the high-conviction threshold of -0.60.
tactical.rationale: The tactical synthesis reads as clearly Bearish USD/JPY because both dossier views reinforce the same directional signal — USD bearish (dossier conviction 2/5) and JPY bullish (dossier conviction 3/5) — creating a double-barrelled pair signal. The two heaviest factors — Policy & Rates (0.24) and Inflation & Growth (0.18) — are both decisively bearish for the pair, contributing -0.276 of the -0.483 total, because rate convergence (BOJ hawkish 6-3 vote + Fed dovish 8-4 vote) and inflation dynamics (US stagflation forces cuts, Japan inflation forces hikes) push USD/JPY in the same direction. The intervention ceiling at 160.72 provides natural risk definition for short positions, capping the pair's upside and creating asymmetric payoff. Risk Appetite and Flows & Positioning are neutral, providing no counterweight — the obvious trade is already on (leveraged funds long JPY, aggregate G10 short USD), which limits squeeze fuel but doesn't reverse direction. The 12 May US CPI is the binary catalyst that could temporarily lift USD/JPY 200–300 pips on a hot print, but cannot change the structural rate-convergence trajectory. Guidance & Credibility adds -0.046 as the BOJ's unprecedented 6-3 hawkish split creates a more credible normalisation signal than any prior Ueda meeting. Fiscal and Market Structure factors add modest bearish contributions, consistent with the broader thesis. The path of least resistance is lower, toward 154–155 before the June 17 BOJ MPM and FOMC resolution.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: USD/JPY ranks as one of the most attractive short positions among all JPY crosses, alongside EUR/JPY and GBP/JPY, because both legs contribute bearish signal. Among all USD shorts, it ranks behind EUR/USD (which captures the largest DXY weight) but ahead of USD/CHF (where CHF overvaluation limits further gains). Among JPY longs, it ranks 1st because the USD-specific bearish overlay amplifies the JPY-bullish signal, creating the strongest bilateral alignment in the G10 complex at the tactical horizon.

conditions: BOJ hawkish hold posture without surprise hike at June 17
base_case.path: USD/JPY grinds lower through the 155.55 May low within 7–10 trading days, consolidates 153–155 ahead of the June 17 cluster, with 80–150 pip daily ranges and spike-and-fade around event days (12 May CPI, 15 May transition, 19 May GDP, 23 May Tokyo CPI). Stair-step descent pattern.

conditions: 12 May CPI prints ≥+0.4% MoM triggering hawkish repricing of Fed path
Iran ceasefire fails with Brent $120 restoring energy-shock yen weakness
BOJ Summary of Opinions reveals 4+ members open to June hike
Iran deal signing firms with Brent toward $85

tactical.path_expectation: The 2–4 week path is most likely a choppy grind lower: spot oscillates in the 154–158 band with the 200-day MA (156.44) as the gravitational centre, drifting lower as the June BOJ MPM approaches. Expect 80–150 pip daily ranges with 2–3 binary headline events including the 12 May CPI, 15 May Powell-Warsh transition, 19 May Japan GDP, and 23 May Tokyo CPI. The pair should test and break the 155.55 May 1 low within 7–10 trading days, then consolidate 153–155 ahead of the June 17 cluster. Pullback levels for re-shorts are the 157.50–158.60 zone. Pattern shape is a stair-step descent with spike-and-fade around event days rather than a clean trending move.

increase_conditions: Increase to 5 if: BOJ delivers June 17 hike AND 12 May CPI ≤3.1% AND Brent <$90
Increase to 4 if: BOJ Summary of Opinions shows 4+ members open to June hike OR Tokyo CPI prints at or above 2.0%
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 2 if: 12 May CPI ≥3.5% AND positioning squeeze lifts USD/JPY above 159
Decrease to 1 if: Iran re-ignition with Brent $130 AND BOJ June dovish hold (no dissent escalation)

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly favourable; the intervention ceiling caps upside loss at ~160 while downside scenarios carry larger payoff (668 pips to 150 in the downside case). Distribution is left-skewed (larger downside payoff than upside loss).

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.586 maps to Conviction 4/5 (strong directional view). Eight factors are bearish, two are neutral (Flows & Positioning, Event Risk), zero are bullish. The score is driven by Policy & Rates (-0.180), External Balance (-0.104), and Guidance & Credibility (-0.084). The score exceeds the tactical score in magnitude, reflecting the stronger structural alignment across longer-horizon factors.
strategic.rationale: The strategic synthesis is decisively Bearish USD/JPY at conviction 4/5 because all structural forces align: rate convergence (BOJ hikes + Fed cuts = ~60bp narrowing), external balance (US twin deficit vs Japan structural surplus), fiscal trajectory (US deteriorating vs Japan stable-on-BOJ-buyer), and institutional credibility (BOJ gaining vs Fed losing). The bilateral interaction creates a signal stronger than either standalone view — USD bearish (conviction 3/5) and JPY bullish (conviction 3/5) compound to conviction 4/5 for the pair because the factors that drive USD weakness (stagflation, fiscal, Fed-independence erosion) simultaneously drive JPY strength (rate convergence, TOT improvement, safe-haven reactivation). External Balance carries the second-highest strategic weight (0.16) and contributes -0.104 to the score — the $29.3T bilateral NIIP differential is the largest in G10 and reinforces rate convergence as the dual structural anchor. Guidance & Credibility at 0.12 weight adds -0.084 as the BOJ's largest-ever CPI projection revision locks in multi-quarter normalisation. The edge versus consensus is timing: we expect ~70% of the strategic move to complete by end-Q3 rather than the gradual H2 decline consensus expects, driven by the BOJ June-July decision cluster and the Iran-ceasefire oil normalisation. Forecasts for late-2026 cluster around ¥144 to ¥148 as the rate differential compresses and Japan's normalisation path becomes more credible. We target 148–152 by end-Q3.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Short USD/JPY ranks as the #1 opportunity among all JPY crosses strategically, and among the top 3 short-USD expressions (alongside long EUR/USD and short USD/CHF). The double-barrelled signal (both legs bearish for the pair) is the strongest bilateral alignment in the G10 complex. The combination of the world's largest bilateral NIIP gap, the highest rate-convergence velocity, and the intervention-defined ceiling creates an unusually well-defined risk/reward profile that is superior to any other USD or JPY cross at the 1–6 month horizon.

conditions: BOJ delivers one hike to 1.00% (June or July)
Iran ceasefire holds with Brent $80–90 by Q3
US recession avoided but growth slows to 1.5–1.8%
base_case.path: Phase 1 (May–June): consolidate 154–158 with intervention ceiling, June 17 BOJ MPM drives 200–300 pip lower. Phase 2 (July–September): directional trend toward 150–153 as BOJ hikes and Fed leans dovish, 55-week EMA (~153.90) broken. Phase 3 (October–November): potential acceleration through 150 on second BOJ hike or Fed cut. Pullback levels: 156 then 158 in Phase 1; 153 then 155 in Phase 2.

conditions: Iran ceasefire fails with Brent $130 sustained through Q3
BOJ holds through 2026; Fed pauses cuts on persistent inflation above 3.5%
BOJ delivers two hikes to 1.25% by September
Brent normalises below $80, US recession materialises

strategic.path_expectation: The 1–6 month path features a phased decline structured around central bank decision clusters. Phase 1 (May–June) sees USD/JPY consolidate 154–158 with the intervention-defined ceiling providing structure, ending with the June 17 BOJ MPM and FOMC resolution driving a potential 200–300 pip directional move lower. Phase 2 (July–September) represents the directional trend phase as the BOJ delivers its expected hike and the Fed leans increasingly dovish — target zone 150–153 with the 55-week EMA (~153.90) as the critical structural level to break for trend confirmation and CTA systematic selling engagement. Phase 3 (October–November) offers potential acceleration through 150 if a second BOJ hike or Fed cut materialises, with the US midterms adding political uncertainty that weighs on USD in the final phase.

increase_conditions: Increase to 5 if: Two BOJ hikes by September AND Fed cuts at least 25bp AND Brent <$80
Increase to 5 if: USD/JPY breaks below 152 sustained for 5+ days on BOJ hawkish confirmation
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 3 if: BOJ delivers one hike but pauses AND Fed holds AND oil stays $95–105 through Q3
Decrease to 2 if: Iran re-ignition sustained with Brent $120+ AND BOJ delays all hikes to 2027
Decrease to 1 if: US exceptionalism narrative re-engages AND USD/JPY breaks 162 sustained

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Strongly favourable; intervention caps upside losses at a well-defined level (~160–161) while BOJ-driven downside is open-ended to 145–148 in the downside scenario. Distribution is significantly left-skewed with the downside scenario payoff (1,068 pips) more than double the upside scenario loss (432 pips).

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Bloomberg consensus median: USD/JPY 153 by end-2026. Strategist range: J.P. Morgan bullish 164, ING gradual decline to 153 by Q4, Scotiabank targets 150. Trading range consensus: roughly 146–154 for 2026.
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: Below consensus — more aggressive yen appreciation than median
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: ~100–500 pips below consensus median at end-Q3 2026 (consensus ~153, we target 148–152)
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We differ from consensus on three specific dimensions: (1) BOJ June hike probability — consensus prices ~35–40%, we estimate ~45–50% based on the unprecedented 6-3 hawkish vote and Ueda's explicit 'real interest rates significantly low' language; (2) Fed easing speed — we see 25–50bp of cuts by September vs consensus pricing one cut by December; (3) Oil normalisation pace — we expect Brent at $80–85 by August vs consensus $90, which accelerates Japan's terms-of-trade improvement and removes the energy-shock headwind that has suppressed yen strength in 2026. The combination of these three factors arriving simultaneously in the June-September window compresses the timeline for the consensus 153 target to be reached (end-Q3 vs end-year).
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: ~200–500 pips of additional alpha beyond consensus path if strategic target of 148–152 reached by end-Q3
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is correct at 153 by year-end, we are only modestly more aggressive (148–152 vs 153) — not a catastrophic miss. The main risk is a timing miss rather than a directional miss.

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical Bearish (conviction 3) and strategic Bearish (conviction 4) are directionally aligned, with strategic conviction higher because the multi-meeting BOJ normalisation path and structural rate convergence provide greater probability of resolution over 1–6 months. The higher strategic conviction reflects the compounding effect of multiple policy meetings, external balance normalisation, and fiscal dynamics that accumulate over time rather than resolving at a single binary event.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Build strategic short USD/JPY positions now; add on pullbacks to 157–158; accelerate on June BOJ hawkish confirmation. The 17 June BOJ MPM is the dominant catalyst that could elevate tactical conviction from 3 to 4; a hawkish BOJ hold would maintain the 3/4 split.

catalysts.rationale: The highest-impact near-term binary event for USD/JPY. A cool print (≤+0.2% MoM) accelerates the downside move toward 153–154 by reinforcing Fed cut pricing and narrowing the bilateral rate differential. A hot print (≥+0.4% MoM) lifts USD/JPY 200–300 pips temporarily via USD rally and short-USD squeeze, but cannot change the structural rate-convergence trajectory. Energy passthrough from the war period creates upside CPI risk. The 12 May print is the fulcrum around which tactical positioning adjusts before the June 17 BOJ-FOMC cluster.

The inaugural public commentary from incoming Fed Chair Warsh sets market expectations for the Fed's reaction function under new leadership. A dovish Warsh surprise (endorsing near-term cuts, downplaying inflation) weakens USD and drives USD/JPY lower. A hawkish surprise (emphasising inflation credibility, delaying cuts) is a tail risk that could push USD/JPY toward 158–160 temporarily. The transition itself adds institutional uncertainty to the USD leg regardless of the tone, as market participants reassess the Fed's reaction function. The Powell-to-Warsh handoff is the most significant US central bank leadership change in seven years.

Japan Q1 GDP directly affects the BOJ reaction function and June hike probability. A weak print could signal BOJ caution and push the June hike probability below 30%, temporarily lifting USD/JPY 100–150 pips. A strong or in-line print reinforces the hawkish hold thesis and supports the June-or-July hike timeline. The BOJ has explicitly acknowledged the growth-inflation trade-off in its April MPM minutes, making this release a genuine binary for the June hike probability rather than a noise print.

Tokyo CPI is the leading indicator for national CPI, typically preceding the national release by 3 weeks and serving as the BOJ's key real-time inflation signal. A reading at or above 2.0% accelerates BOJ June hike probability from ~35–40% toward 50%+ and directly pressures USD/JPY lower. Consensus expects ~1.5–1.7%, creating asymmetric upside risk from persistent services inflation and the Shunto wage passthrough. A reading below 1.5% would briefly lift USD/JPY by reducing June hike urgency.

The ECB decision affects USD/JPY indirectly through EUR/JPY cross-rate dynamics and broad USD positioning. A hawkish ECB hold that narrows the UST-Bund spread further contributes to broad USD weakness that spills over into USD/JPY. The direct bilateral transmission for USD/JPY is modest, but a surprise hawkish ECB outcome could catalyse a broader USD selling wave that lifts USD/JPY lower by 50–100 pips as a secondary effect.

The single most important catalyst for USD/JPY in the tactical horizon. Three April MPM dissenters already voted for 1.00%; Ueda's explicit 'real interest rates significantly low' language is near-certain hawkish signalling. A hike to 1.00% with 5-4 or higher majority shocks USD/JPY 200–300 pips lower immediately, with further follow-through as carry positions unwind. A hold maintaining the same 6-3 dissent structure would validate the July hike consensus and limit downside to 50–100 pips. Any reduction in hawkish dissent (below 6-3) would be the true dovish surprise and could lift USD/JPY 150–200 pips temporarily. We assign ~45–50% hike probability vs market-implied 35–40%.

catalysts.event: FOMC Decision + New Summary of Economic Projections
catalysts.rationale: Warsh's first FOMC as Chair with a new dot plot. A dovish revision (median dot to 3.125%, adding one cut for 2026) crystallises the USD bearish thesis and compounds any BOJ hawkishness on the same day. A hold at 3.375% dots with hawkish rhetoric is the upside USD risk but faces the constraint of 8-4 institutional fracture. The June 16–17 consecutive BOJ-FOMC cluster is the most asymmetric bilateral event pair of the tactical horizon: both events can drive USD/JPY in the same direction simultaneously, potentially creating a 400–500 pip compound move.

catalysts.event: BOJ Monetary Policy Meeting with Outlook Report — CRITICAL
catalysts.rationale: If June was a hold, July becomes the dominant hike candidate. The July meeting includes the full Outlook Report with revised growth and inflation projections — historically the setting where the BOJ makes its most consequential decisions. Combined June-or-July hike probability is ~70%, meaning if USD/JPY has not already adjusted for a June hike, July provides a second window. A July hike with an upward revision to the FY2026 CPI forecast (already at 2.8%) would drive USD/JPY toward 150–152 in the near term, triggering Phase 2 of the strategic decline.

H2 Fed cut probability is concentrated in the September meeting. A 25bp cut to 3.25–3.50% compresses the bilateral rate gap from ~287bp toward ~260–240bp (depending on BOJ progress), reinforcing the rate-convergence thesis and adding structural USD selling. Combined with an expected second BOJ hike, this meeting could drive USD/JPY through 150, activating the Phase 3 acceleration scenario and engaging systematic CTA and model selling flows below that psychological level.

Trump's 22% approval on cost-of-living makes Republican losses likely, increasing fiscal-political deadlock and reducing the administration's ability to implement growth-positive policy. USD typically weakens on reduced fiscal policy credibility and increased political uncertainty. This is a secondary USD-negative catalyst that adds to the structural bearish thesis rather than serving as a primary driver. If Republicans lose the House, the debt ceiling and fiscal trajectory become contested, adding term premium to USTs in a USD-negative manner.

invalidators.condition: Brent breaks $130 sustained for 5+ trading days (Iran ceasefire failure)
invalidators.mechanism: Japan's terms-of-trade collapse (95.9% ME oil dependence) would dominate the BOJ rate channel, driving yen weakness through deteriorating current account and intervention dry powder erosion. The energy-shock mechanism overwhelms rate convergence when oil is this elevated.

invalidators.condition: BOJ June 17 MPM delivers dovish hold (8-1 vote or lower dissent count, removal of 'real rates significantly low' language)
invalidators.mechanism: The hawkish-pivot thesis is invalidated and rate convergence slows materially. The market would rapidly reprice June hike probability to near zero and push July probability below 30%, removing the primary near-term catalyst for USD/JPY decline. Leveraged longs in JPY would face an unwind trigger.

invalidators.condition: US 10Y breaks 4.7% sustained for 10+ trading days on hawkish Fed repricing
invalidators.mechanism: The US-Japan 10Y spread re-widens past 220bp, restoring USD/JPY upside from renewed carry attractiveness. Higher US real yields attract global capital flows into USD, overwhelming the BOJ normalisation signal and temporarily reversing the rate-convergence thesis.

invalidators.condition: CFTC leveraged funds net long JPY exceeds +100,000 contracts AND USD/JPY fails to break 152
invalidators.mechanism: Overcrowded long-JPY positioning creates dominant squeeze risk that overwhelms fundamentals. If the pair cannot break 152 despite the crowded long position, the failure creates a squeeze trigger that could unwind 50–70k contracts worth of positioning, driving USD/JPY back toward 158–160.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: CFTC weekly TFF leveraged funds JPY net contracts

invalidators.condition: April CPI prints ≥+0.4% MoM AND core ≥+0.3% MoM (12 May release)
invalidators.mechanism: Hot prints invalidate Fed cut pricing for June and July, triggering short-USD positioning squeeze. The hot CPI narrative temporarily overrides the rate-convergence thesis by pushing back Fed easing timelines by 3–6 months, removing the near-term catalyst for USD/JPY decline without changing the structural direction.

invalidators.condition: Trump announces ≥10% tariffs on Japanese auto imports
invalidators.mechanism: Japan growth shock forces BOJ caution and potentially reverses normalisation timeline. Auto exports represent a significant portion of Japan's trade surplus; a 10%+ tariff would materially compress GDP growth and reduce BOJ confidence in the inflation outlook, pushing the hike timeline from Q3 2026 to 2027. The direct trade impact and BOJ policy response are both JPY-negative.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: USTR publications and White House trade announcements

signposts.bilateral_importance: The primary mechanistic driver of USD/JPY; rate convergence is the dominant thesis and this spread is the single most direct measure of the bilateral rate differential that determines carry attractiveness and institutional flow direction

Asymmetric bilateral impact: Japan imports 95.9% of crude oil from the Middle East while the US is a net oil exporter, creating the largest bilateral energy-vulnerability asymmetry in the G10 complex — each $10/bbl move has disproportionately larger impact on JPY than USD

signposts.indicator: CFTC TFF Leveraged Funds JPY Net Contracts
signposts.confirms_if: Sustained in 40,000–80,000 range with weekly stability
signposts.invalidates_if: Surges above +100,000 (overcrowded) OR reverts to net short below +10,000
signposts.bilateral_importance: Both legs of USD/JPY are positioned for the pair to fall; monitoring JPY positioning specifically provides advance warning of the squeeze risk that could temporarily lift USD/JPY 4–7 figures regardless of fundamentals, as demonstrated in the 2024 violent reversal from -180k extreme shorts

The asymmetric binary catalyst for USD/JPY direction — market is underpricing the June hike at 35–40% vs our estimate of 45–50%, so repricing toward 50%+ represents the primary edge; a BOJ June hike would shock USD/JPY 200–300 pips lower and activate Phase 1 of the strategic decline

signposts.confirms_if: Spot breaks below and stays below for 5+ consecutive trading days
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot rallies above 158.88 (50-day SMA) sustained for 3+ days
signposts.bilateral_importance: The structural trend pivot for USD/JPY — CTA and systematic model flows engage on a decisive break below, creating a self-reinforcing directional move; the current configuration (spot just above 200d at 156.68) represents the critical inflection point that determines whether the pair enters a technical downtrend or consolidates above

signposts.confirms_if: Sustained at or above 2.0% for 2+ consecutive months
signposts.bilateral_importance: Directly drives the BOJ reaction function and the rate convergence thesis — the BOJ has explicitly stated hikes are contingent on inflation remaining sustainably at 2%; above-target persistence with Shunto 5.09% wage lock-in creates the conditions for multi-hike normalisation that underpins the strategic bearish USD/JPY thesis

assumptions: USD/JPY forward points estimated from the bilateral rate differential (~287bp) using standard interest rate parity; precise forward market quotes not directly retrieved and may differ from market-quoted outright rates by 1–5 pips.

BOJ June hike probability assessed at 45–50% based on the three-way April dissent and Ueda's explicit hawkish language; this exceeds market-implied pricing of ~35–40% and is the primary source of our edge versus consensus.

Intervention analysis treats the 160.72 level as a credible ceiling based on the May 1 ¥5.4T deployment, consistent with the 2024 pattern of MOF acting at similar levels with similar firepower. Actual firepower and willingness may differ.

Weight adjustments are minimal (+0.03 to Risk Appetite as a pure risk-proxy pair; -0.03 from USD Liquidity since carry dynamics are captured in Factor 1), with rationale documented in the synthesis section.
Carry-to-vol calculation uses annualised carry of ~287bp divided by 1M IV of ~9.5% = 0.30; threshold of 0.50 for carry attractiveness derived from historical G10 carry-trade profitability research.

PPP fair value of USD/JPY ~95 uses OECD comparative price levels; the extreme 65% deviation is structural/strategic context, not a tactical trading signal and does not influence the near-term targets.

Iran ceasefire probabilities are subjective Bayesian assessments based on available news as of 9 May 2026: 75% holding tactically, 75% strategically, with re-ignition tail at ~25%. These are not derived from options or prediction markets.
Nikkei VI level of ~29.5 is an estimate based on contextual references in source materials; precise current level may differ.

The strategic weight for Factor 10 (USD Liquidity) is adjusted to 0.09 from 0.12 (not 0.08 as in tactical) to reflect the longer-horizon relevance of USD funding conditions; the markdown table showed strategic default 0.12 adjusted to 0.09.
